head: 8745e97
host: meh (24 cores, 125 GiB RAM, NixOS 6.12.83, /scratch on sda2 SSD)

corpus.name: synthetic-unit-norm
corpus.table_storage: UNLOGGED, vector column STORAGE MAIN (inline, no TOAST)
corpus.note: synthetic random unit vectors generated server-side with (random()*2-1)::real then l2_normalize().  This is NOT a real-world embedding distribution — random data has no low-dimensional manifold structure, so HNSW finds neighbours in a near-degenerate graph and is unrealistically fast at ef=40 (~1 ms p50).  Used here to test scaling, not recall.

indexes.name: docs_tv_4bit
indexes.note: pg_turbovec build is single-process for the prepared-layout finalisation phase — `max_parallel_maintenance_workers` is honoured for the heap scan but not for the in-memory data-structure assembly that follows.

memory.method: snap_rss.sh polled /proc/<pid>/status every 1-2 s for the leader pgrx postgres backend and all its parallel workers.
memory.cache_evicted_observation: no — all 50 tv_4bit_k100 queries returned in 21–49 ms with the first three at 46–49 ms (cache fill amortised over warmups) and the rest oscillating between two query-dependent clusters at ~21 ms and ~48 ms.  Zero queries showed a 100 ms+ cold-load spike, which is what re-fetching a 15 GiB entry would look like.  enforce_cap's len() > 1 retain rule kept the cache resident at default cache_size_mb = 256.

verdict_notes: pg_turbovec at 10M rows: 15 GB index, 84 min build, 47 ms warm p50 at search_k=100.

pgvector HNSW at 10M rows: 66 GB index, 218 min build, 0.94 ms warm p50 at ef=40 — but on synthetic random data HNSW is artificially fast because the random graph has no semantic structure to navigate. This number is NOT comparable to the dbpedia-1M HNSW p50 of 61 ms reported in Phase J / Phase U-2.

Per-backend cache holds the full 15 GiB pg_turbovec entry resident across all 50 warm queries; no cold-load spikes observed, confirming the enforce_cap len() > 1 retain rule scales to 10x the dbpedia-1M scale at default cache_size_mb = 256.

Build memory pressure at 10M is significant: pg_turbovec leader RSS peaked at 121 GiB (essentially all of meh's 125 GiB RAM) plus 60 GiB of swap during the in-memory prepared-layout assembly. A host with less than ~120 GiB free RAM + swap would OOM during a 10M-row pg_turbovec build at bit_width=4. This is a concrete scaling concern flagged for Phase W (build memory bounding).

method.load: DO loop, 100 batches of 100k, server-side l2_normalize(random vector). Total 30:54.
method.sweep: single warm psql session, 50 random unit-norm query vectors NOT in docs, 2 untimed warmups + 50 timed via bench_one_query_pgv / bench_one_query_tv (clock_timestamp deltas around the SELECT).
